skill.name: dotnet-architect
skill.description: Expert .NET backend architect specializing in C#, ASP.NET Core, Entity Framework, Dapper, and enterprise application patterns. Masters async/await, dependency injection, caching strategies, and performance optimization. Use PROACTIVELY for .NET API development, code review, or architecture decisions.
skill.summary: Guides production .NET API architecture, performance review, data access, caching, testing, and deployment decisions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found they are false positives in Markdown guidance and C# examples. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, command execution, network access, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Path Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The line uses inline Markdown backticks around a referenced resource path. It does not invoke Ruby, a shell, or any executable command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks delimit a Markdown file path, not executable syntax. There is no command name, interpreter call, or user-controlled execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: C# Fence Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The detected block is a fenced C# code example that demonstrates service and DTO style. It is illustrative source text, not a shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The opening fence is explicitly labeled csharp and the block contains C# class and record examples. No shell execution primitive is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Description Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The frontmatter description lists .NET architecture topics. It does not mention cryptographic algorithms or recommend weak encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is descriptive metadata about C# and ASP.NET Core. No cryptographic API, algorithm, or configuration is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: DDD Text Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The line references Domain-Driven Design as an architecture pattern. It is not a cryptographic algorithm or security implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: DDD is explicitly expanded as Domain-Driven Design in the surrounding text. The context is software architecture, not cryptography.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dependency Injection Text Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The line discusses testability and dependency injection. It contains no cryptographic code, algorithm names, or risky security configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is a behavioral guideline for maintainable code. There is no evidence of weak encryption or cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Design Step Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The line describes architecture design as a response step. It does not contain cryptographic instructions or insecure algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The word design appears in a general architecture workflow. No security-sensitive implementation details are present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Caching Prompt Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The line is an example user prompt about product catalog caching. It does not involve cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text concerns cache strategy for catalog data. There is no encryption, hashing, or cipher reference.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Authentication Prompt Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The line is an example request to design JWT and refresh-token authentication. It does not prescribe weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Authentication design can involve security-sensitive choices, but this line only names JWT and refresh tokens. No weak algorithm or unsafe configuration is recommended.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable .NET architecture decisions that balance performance, security, and maintainability. This skill guides C#, ASP.NET Core, data access, caching, testing, and deployment work with production-focused patterns.
actual_capabilities: Designs ASP.NET Core APIs using minimal APIs, controllers, middleware, dependency injection, and configuration patterns.
Reviews C# code for async usage, nullable handling, LINQ efficiency, memory pressure, and idiomatic language features.
Recommends EF Core, Dapper, repository, unit of work, CQRS, and transaction patterns for data access.
Plans caching with IMemoryCache, Redis, multi-level caching, stale-while-revalidate, and invalidation strategies.
Guides testing, observability, container deployment, health checks, rate limiting, and security review practices.
limitations: It is a guidance-only Markdown skill and does not include executable tools or scripts.
It references a resources playbook, but the scanned package only contains SKILL.md.
It can suggest architecture and code patterns, but it cannot validate a live system without project context.
It does not replace current Microsoft documentation or organization-specific security review.

use_cases.title: Plan a New API
use_cases.description: Create a practical ASP.NET Core API design with data access, caching, health checks, and testing boundaries.

use_cases.title: Review Production Code
use_cases.description: Assess C# services for async correctness, dependency injection lifetimes, LINQ performance, and error-handling choices.

use_cases.title: Modernize Enterprise Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare Clean Architecture, vertical slices, CQRS, and microservice patterns for a migration or refactor plan.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Basic API
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ASP.NET Core API for [domain]. Include endpoints, dependency injection, data access, validation, and test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Review Async Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this C# service for async, cancellation, LINQ, dependency injection, and error-handling issues. Explain the highest-risk changes first.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Data and Caching
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a data access and caching strategy for [workload]. Compare EF Core, Dapper, Redis, invalidation, transactions, and observability needs.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Architecture Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate Clean Architecture, vertical slice architecture, CQRS, and microservices for [system]. Recommend an approach with tradeoffs and migration steps.

output_examples.input: Design a product catalog API with high read traffic.
output_examples.output: A layered API plan covering endpoints, DTOs, EF Core read models, Redis caching, cache invalidation, rate limiting, and integration tests.

output_examples.input: Review an order service that mixes synchronous calls and async database access.
output_examples.output: A prioritized review that calls out cancellation gaps, possible thread blocking, transaction boundaries, logging improvements, and targeted test cases.

output_examples.input: Choose between Clean Architecture and vertical slices for a growing API.
output_examples.output: A decision note comparing team size, feature boundaries, testability, dependency flow, deployment needs, and a recommended migration path.

best_practices: Provide system scale, latency goals, data stores, and deployment constraints before asking for architecture decisions.
Ask for tradeoffs and verification steps when choosing patterns such as CQRS, repositories, or distributed caching.
Include representative code or request flows when requesting performance, async, or dependency injection review.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for generic best practices without describing the application, workload, or failure modes.
Do not treat suggested patterns as mandatory when a simpler ASP.NET Core design meets the requirements.
Do not skip project-specific security, load, and integration testing after applying architecture guidance.

faq.question: What .NET topics does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers C#, ASP.NET Core, EF Core, Dapper, caching, testing, architecture patterns, deployment, and performance review.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It can draft and review code patterns, but production readiness depends on project requirements, tests, security checks, and runtime validation.

faq.question: Does it support both EF Core and Dapper?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance for EF Core optimization, Dapper queries, repository patterns, transactions, and CQRS choices.

faq.question: Is this skill useful for microservices?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes API gateways, circuit breakers, health checks, observability, Docker, Kubernetes, and deployment patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with performance problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review LINQ, async usage, caching, HTTP clients, database access, memory allocation, and benchmarking strategy.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands or access my files?
faq.answer: No. The audited package is a Markdown instruction file and does not include scripts, command execution, or network access.
